encounters.name: The Death Knight Ambush
encounters.description:
## The Death Knight Ambush

**Location:** The Outer Tunnels — narrow, crumbling passages that connect the surface to the inner ruins.

The party descends into the Netherese ruins through a series of narrow tunnels carved from ancient black stone. The air grows cold and thick with the scent of decay. Flickering arcane lights cast long, dancing shadows across walls etched with Netherese runes.

As the party navigates a particularly narrow chokepoint, the ground trembles. From the darkness ahead, six armored figures emerge — Death Knights of Netheril, their armor still bearing the sigils of the fallen empire. Their eyes burn with unholy green fire. Behind them, eight Specters drift silently from the shadows, and four Ghosts phase through the walls. A Marilith, its six serpentine tails coiling behind it, steps from a side passage — the tactician leading this ambush.

The Death Knights charge immediately, using their legendary actions to reposition and cut off the party's retreat. The Marilith takes a high position and begins targeting spellcasters with its multiple attacks. The Specters and Ghosts spread out, forcing the party to divide their attention.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The narrow tunnels limit the party's formation — front-line characters are funneled into a killing zone
- The Death Knights focus fire on the party's front line while the Marilith targets spellcasters from range
- Specters and Ghosts require specific damage types (radiant or magic weapons) to defeat efficiently
- A successful DC 15 Perception check reveals the Marilith's hiding position before combat begins
- The secret entrance (if discovered) allows the party to bypass this encounter entirely

encounters.name: The Balor's Descent
encounters.description:
## The Balor's Descent

**Location:** The Grand Hall — a vast chamber that once served as the Netherese enclave's central gathering place.

The party emerges from the tunnels into a cavernous hall so vast that its ceiling is lost in darkness. The floor is cracked and uneven, with fissures glowing with molten light below. The air is hot and smells of sulfur. Massive pillars, each wide enough to house a tree, support the ceiling — but many have crumbled, leaving jagged stumps.

Suddenly, the darkness above erupts. Two Balors crash down from the ceiling, their massive wings creating shockwaves that send debris flying. Each Balor is thirty feet tall, clad in black armor, wielding a massive whip and a greatsword wreathed in flame. Behind them, four Erutuki — fiendish creatures with the bodies of lions and the wings of bats — swoop down, their screeches echoing through the hall. A swarm of twelve Fire Newts pours from the fissures in the floor, creating walls of flame that分割 the battlefield.

The Balors use their fly speed to stay airborne, targeting isolated party members with their whips and breathing fire across the hall. The Erutuki provide additional aerial pressure, using their bite and claw attacks to harass the party's flanks. The Fire Newts are essentially cannon fodder but their fire breath creates hazardous terrain that forces the party to keep moving.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The Balors should use their fly speed to stay airborne and target isolated party members
- The Erutuki's fear aura (DC 18 Wisdom save) can disrupt the party's formation
- Fire Newts create difficult terrain and deal fire damage on approach — area attacks clear them quickly
- The crumbling pillars can be destroyed (AC 15, 25 HP) to create cover or collapse sections of the ceiling
- If the party has strong area control, the Balors may attempt to retreat and regroup through a passage in the eastern wall

encounters.name: The Dragon and the Worm
encounters.description:
## The Dragon and the Worm

**Location:** The Chasm Chamber — a massive underground cavern with a deep chasm splitting the floor in two.

The party reaches a natural cavern so large that it defies comprehension. A chasm, easily two hundred feet wide, splits the chamber in half. A narrow stone bridge — the only crossing — spans the gap. Below, the chasm drops into darkness, but the party can hear the sound of something massive moving in the depths.

On the far side of the chasm, an Adult Red Dragon perches on a ledge of black rock, its scales gleaming like polished ruby in the dim light. Its golden eyes fix on the party with predatory intelligence. Beside it, the ground trembles as a Purple Worm emerges from a tunnel — its segmented body easily forty feet long, its maw a circular nightmare of grinding teeth.

Six Magma Mephits swirl around the dragon like living embers, their presence making the air shimmer with heat. The dragon speaks in a voice like grinding stone: "Mortals. You dare enter the domain of Valandril? Very well. Die, and your bones will feed the earth."

The dragon opens with a cone of fire across the bridge, forcing the party to scatter. The Purple Worm charges, attempting to swallow characters whole and drag them into the chasm below. The Magma Mephits use their fire breath and self-destruct abilities to harass the party from range.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The bridge is only 10 feet wide — characters can be pushed into the chasm (DC 20 Athletics to climb out)
- The dragon's lair action can cause rockfalls from the ceiling, dealing 3d10 bludgeoning damage
- The Purple Worm's Engulf ability forces a DC 18 Dexterity save or be swallowed whole (trapped inside takes 31 acid damage per turn)
- Destroying the Magma Mephits releases a burst of fire (2d6 fire damage to nearby creatures)
- The chasm contains hidden tunnels — a successful DC 20 Investigation check reveals a secret passage beneath the bridge
- If the party defeats the dragon first, the Purple Worm becomes frightened and flees

encounters.name: The Aboleth's Domain
encounters.description:
## The Aboleth's Domain

**Location:** An underground aquatic cavern — one of the crown fragment hiding places.

The party descends through a flooded tunnel into a vast underground lake. The water is dark and still, reflecting the bioluminescent fungi that cling to the cavern walls. The air is thick with humidity and the smell of ancient decay. Strange shapes move beneath the surface.

As the party navigates a narrow ledge along the cavern wall, the water erupts. Two Aboleths rise from the depths — ancient, eyeless horrors with wrinkled gray skin and tentacles hanging from their bellies. Their very presence radiates an overwhelming psychic pressure. Behind them, a cult of Kuo-toa emerges from hidden caves — ten fanatical warriors wielding tridents and nets, accompanied by three Kuo-toa Whips who crack their whip-like appendages with menacing cracks. Two Chuul, their pincers dripping with venom, clamp onto the cavern ceiling and drop down.

The Aboleths use their Psychic Crush and Enslave abilities immediately, targeting the party's strongest members. The Kuo-toa swarm from all directions, using their nets to restrain characters and drag them into the water. The Chuul focus on isolating single targets with their venomous pincers.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The Aboleth's Enslave ability (DC 18 Wisdom save) is a major threat — enslaved characters act on the Aboleth's turn
- Psychic Crush deals 22 psychic damage in a 10-foot cube — area control is essential
- Kuo-toa in water gain advantage on attacks and can hold their breath for hours
- The cavern contains shallow areas (5 feet deep) where characters can stand and avoid being pulled underwater
- A successful DC 15 Religion check reveals that the crown fragment is housed in a crystal sphere at the cavern's center
- Destroying the crystal sphere releases a wave of force damage (4d10) but also destroys the fragment

encounters.name: The Demilich's Vault
encounters.description:
## The Demilich's Vault

**Location:** An ancient necropolis — another crown fragment hiding place.

The party enters a vast underground necropolis, its corridors lined with sarcophagi and statues of forgotten nobles. The air is cold and still, and the only sound is the crunch of bone-dust underfoot. Strange runes glow faintly on the walls, pulsing with necromantic energy.

As the party crosses into the central chamber, the sarcophagi begin to rattle. From them emerge six Wights, their armor rusted and tattered, their eyes burning with undead hunger. Four Mummies step from the shadows, their bandaged forms reeking of decay and their touch carrying the curse of undeath. Above, ten Specters drift from the ceiling, their wails filling the chamber with an atmosphere of dread.

And then the Demilich reveals itself — a floating, eyeless skull wreathed in necrotic energy, its single magical eye glowing with malevolent intelligence. It speaks in a voice that seems to come from everywhere at once: "You seek the fragment? Then prove yourselves worthy of death."

The Demilich opens with its Anti-Magic Ray, targeting the party's most powerful spellcasters. The Wights charge the front line while the Mummies use their Curse ability to weaken the party's defenders. The Specters harass from range, and the Demilich's Paralyzing Ray forces characters to make difficult saving throws.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The Demilich's Anti-Magic Ray creates a 15-foot cone of anti-magic — spellcasters must stay out of it
- Mummy's Curse (DC 17 Constitution save) reduces max HP on a failed save — a persistent threat
- Wights can only be hit by magic weapons, forcing the party to adapt their tactics
- The sarcophagi can be destroyed (AC 13, 10 HP) to release trapped spirits that attack the undead (friendly fire to enemies)
- A successful DC 18 Arcana check reveals the Demilich's phylactery is hidden in one of the side chambers
- Destroying the phylactery deals 10d10 necrotic damage to the Demilich and prevents it from reforming

encounters.name: The Androsphinx's Trial
encounters.description:
## The Androsphinx's Trial

**Location:** A hidden temple deep beneath the earth — the final crown fragment's guardian.

The party discovers a beautifully preserved temple carved from white marble, its walls adorned with paintings depicting the creation of the Crown of Netheril. The air is warm and smells of incense. Golden light streams from unseen sources, illuminating a vast chamber with a raised dais at its center.

As the party approaches the dais, a massive figure rises from behind it. An Androsphinx — the body of a lion, the wings of an eagle, and the head and breasts of a beautiful woman — regards them with ancient, knowing eyes. Its voice is melodic and carries an undeniable authority.

"I am the Guardian of the Seventh Fragment. I have waited ten thousand years for worthy challengers. You may pass, but only if you prove your wisdom, your strength, and your courage. Fail, and you will join the statues that line my temple."

The sphinx presents three challenges:

1. **Riddle of Wisdom** — The sphinx poses a riddle tied to Netherese history. A failed DC 18 Intelligence (Arcana or History) check deals 4d10 psychic damage as the sphinx's magic lashes out.

2. **Trial of Strength** — Two Stone Golems rise from the floor, attacking the party. The sphinx watches silently. The golems are CR 9 each and serve as a combat test.

3. **Trial of Courage** — The sphinx asks each party member to declare their motivation for seeking the crown. Those who speak with genuine selflessness gain advantage on all saving throws for the remainder of the encounter. Those who speak with greed or ambition suffer disadvantage.

**Tactical Notes:**
- The sphinx is not hostile unless the party attacks first — it values honor and fair play
- The Stone Golems have Damage Resistance from nonmagical attacks and can immobilize characters with their Slam attack
- Successfully completing all three challenges causes the sphinx to yield the fragment willingly and grant the party a blessing (advantage on one saving throw per short rest for 24 hours)
- The statues lining the temple are former challengers — a successful DC 20 Investigation check reveals one is still alive and can be freed with a Wish or similar magic
- The sphinx knows secrets about Valandril's true plan and the Tarrasque's awakening

encounters.name: The Final Confrontation — Valandril's Last Stand
encounters.description:
## The Final Confrontation — Valandril's Last Stand

**Location:** The Crown Chamber — the innermost chamber of the Netherese ruins.

The party bursts through the final doors into the Crown Chamber — a vast circular chamber where seven crystalline fragments hover above a central dais, slowly rotating in an arcane dance. The air shimmers with distorted light, and the ground is covered in glowing Netherese runes that pulse with each fragment's energy.

At the chamber's eastern end, seated upon a throne of black stone, is Valandril the Undying. His skeletal form is draped in tattered robes of deep purple and gold. Seven crystalline shards float around him — the fragments he has already collected. Above his skull, a crown of jagged arcane energy hovers, incomplete but growing. His eye sockets burn with violet flame.

Behind him, his elite guard assembles: three Death Knights step from the shadows, their armor gleaming. A Marilith coils behind the throne, her six tails twitching with anticipation. And then the ground shakes as an Ancient Red Dragon crashes through the western wall — Valandril's final ace. Twelve Specters and six Ghosts phase through the walls, filling the chamber with undead dread.

Valandril rises from his throne, his voice echoing with the weight of ten thousand years. "You are too late. The crown is nearly whole. With it, I will restore Netheril to the skies and remake this world in the image of its greatest age. Join me, and you shall share in the glory of the new empire. Refuse, and die with the rest of this decaying age."

**Phase 1 (Valandril at full HP):** Valandril uses Counterspell and Dispel Magic to neutralize spellcasters. Death Knights protect him while the Marilith and Dragon attack from range. Specters and Ghosts harass the party's flanks.

**Phase 2 (Valandril below 50% HP):** Valandril activates Contingency — he teleports to the central dais and begins the reassembly ritual. The party must stop him or face a dramatically different final encounter. The Dragon and Marilith fight desperately to protect him.

**Phase 3 (If reassembly completes):** The crown partially reassembles. Valandril gains temporary legendary actions, his spells deal extra damage, and the chamber's reality-warping effects intensify (random teleportation, environmental hazards).

**Tactical Notes:**
- The seven pedestals can be used strategically — placing a fragment on a pedestal triggers different effects
- The floating shards provide half-cover and can be destroyed (AC 15, 20 HP) to release 4d10 force damage
- Reality Warp lair action (initiative 20) teleports random creatures — plan positioning carefully
- If the party has collected fragments, each fragment reduces Valandril's power (fewer allies, fewer charges)
- Myranda may intervene if the party shows mercy to cult members — she turns on Valandril
- The chamber's runes can be activated (DC 18 Arcana) to create barriers or deal radiant damage to undead

encounters.name: The Tarrasque Awakens — Optional Finale
encounters.description:
## The Tarrasque Awakens — Optional Finale

**Location:** The surface above Waterdeep — if the party fails to stop Valandril's ritual.

If the party takes too long, or if Valandril completes the reassembly ritual, the crown's power triggers an unintended consequence. Valandril's divinations were correct — the crown's energy has awakened the Tarrasque, the ultimate creature of destruction, from its slumber beneath the Earth. But Valandril did not anticipate this. His eyes widen with horror as the ground beneath Waterdeep begins to crack.

The Tarrasque erupts from the earth in a cataclysmic explosion of rock and fire. Three hundred feet of armored destruction, its single eye burning with mindless hunger. Its very presence causes earthquakes. Buildings crumble. The sky darkens with ash.

But the Tarrasque is not Valandril's ally — it is a force of pure destruction. Six Erutuki and twenty Fire Newts, drawn by the crown's energy, swarm around the beast, either trying to ride it or flee from it. The Tarrasque's Devour ability can consume entire buildings — and characters.

Valandril, realizing his catastrophic error, offers a desperate bargain: "Help me stop this thing, and I will surrender the crown and all its power to you. Fail, and we all die together."

**Tactical Notes:**
- This is an optional encounter — only trigger if the party fails to stop Valandril in time
- The Tarrasque is essentially an environmental hazard — it moves 40 feet per turn, destroying everything in its path
- Its Immune to all damage except magic weapons and spells makes it nearly impossible to kill conventionally
- The crown's power can be used to seal the Tarrasque back underground — but this requires all seven fragments and a DC 20 Arcana check
- If the party has Myranda's Ring of Spell Turning, it can reflect the Tarrasque's Magic Resistance
- The Erutuki and Fire Newts can be used as distractions — they attack the Tarrasque but deal negligible damage
- This encounter is designed to be unwinnable through combat alone — the party must use the crown's power creatively
- If the party succeeds in resealing the Tarrasque, they earn the lasting gratitude of all of Faerûn
